notes.title: 2026 NVIDIA GTC
notes.content:
# Untitled Note

## [Pregame](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RTmSrIFZanc) 
- mostly applications of AI and notes from major CEO's that are using this infrastructure

  - [Perplexity Computer](https://www.perplexity.ai/hub/blog/introducing-perplexity-computer)

- somehow using all of the data in enterpise companies that is not structured and somehow utilizing it since it can be used for training, and other things

- good speakers talk very high level, keeping things at the highest abstraction level and not going too granular

- almost everything in the 5 layer cake is being transforemed by AI

## [5 layer cake of ai by Jenseng huang](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/ai-5-layer-cake/)

  - ![Screenshot 2026-03-16 at 12 10 26 PM](/notes/images/Screenshot_2026-03-16_at_12_10_26_PM.png)

## co-design: basically desining with all teams tightly coupled together and working together at the same time instead of teams working semi-independently then sharing what is completed.


## Questions:
  - How does edge ai work and how is it possible
    - more possible with hardware acceleration with GPUs and NPUs
    - **model quantization**: reducing the FP precision (ex from FP16 to FP8) to reduce size of the models parameters/weights
    - most LLMs are within the 1-8 trillion parameter range as of 3/17/26]
  - lpddr5: low-power double data (transfer data on both rise and fall clock edge) rate RAM
  - NPU vs TPU
    - TPUs are architected for extreme throughput and used in data centers, and use structures like systolic arrays and a lot of HBM (high bandwidth memory)
    - NPUs are architected for edge devices like smartphones and IoT devices. These are made for extreme power efficiency and shared memory (TPUs have their own memory).
  - [vera rubin platform semianalysis](https://newsletter.semianalysis.com/p/vera-rubin-extreme-co-design-an-evolution)
    - ![image](/notes/images/image-4.png)
    - ahhhh
    - Adaptive compression: removes all zeros from the data stream needed to be processed, basically removing all unnecessary computation, leading to speedup. For example, if we have an image to process with majority of it black (0), we can remove all of the computation for it since x * 0 = 0.
      - VERA! ![image](/notes/images/image-5.png)
    - [SIMT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_instruction,_multiple_threads): Single instruction multiple threads - basically sending one instruction to multiple processing units (threads) to compute their own thread of computation.
'''
The "ID Card" System (Implicit Distribution)

When you launch a program on a GPU (a "Kernel"), you aren't just starting one process; you are spawning thousands or millions of threads. The GPU hardware automatically assigns each thread a set of coordinates, similar to a seat number in a stadium.

-   threadIdx: Its position in a small "Block" (e.g., Seat 5).
    
-   blockIdx: The "Section" of the stadium (e.g., Section 2).

Because every thread knows its own coordinates, you can write one line of code that works for everyone:

When the "broadcast" instruction arrives saying **"MULTIPLY YOUR DATA BY 2,"** Thread #5 looks at input_array(5), while Thread #500 looks at input_array(500). They are following the same command, but their "eyes" are looking at different spots in memory.
'''

  - 6th gen nvlink!
  - nvlink switches![image  10](/notes/images/image_10_.png)![image  20](/notes/images/image_20_.png)
    - Basically what connects all of the GPUs together for GPU-to-GPU communication
  -   ConnectX-9: connecting one compute tray node to another
  -   BlueField-4 (DPU): sits in line with connectx-9 and acts as traffic controller between tray nodes
  -   SOCAMM: Small Outline Compression Attached Memory Module -  basically next gen of LPDDR5X
  - Tensor Core
    - MMA operation (Matrix Multiply accumulate): operation that performs A \* B + C, a fundamental operation in ML for matrix multiplication
    - [SM]([https://modal.com/gpu-glossary/device-hardware/streaming-multiprocessor](https://modal.com/gpu-glossary/device-hardware/streaming-multiprocessor)) (streaming multiprocessor): similar to a CPU core, just optimized for extreme parallel work and throughput![*Blackwell Ultra SM architecture*](/notes/images/image_12_.png)
    - Cuda Core vs Tensor Core: cuda core is for general purpose parallel work such as graphics, physics, AI whereas tensor core is for parallel work specifically tailored towards AI, ML/DL, matrix multiplication (MMA ops)
    - [Standford CS149]([https://gfxcourses.stanford.edu/cs149/fall25/lecture/gpuarch](https://gfxcourses.stanford.edu/cs149/fall25/lecture/gpuarch)): GPU Architecture and CUDA Programming
  - CUDA
  - HBM4: high bandwidth memory 4
    - much higher data bus (wider highway) allowing for higher bandwidth
      - [usage in inference](https://newsletter.semianalysis.com/i/174558655/hbm-usage-in-inference)
        - model weights and kv cache sit in HBW. Each token transaction, GPU reads weights and KV-cache, computes token through the NN, then stores the generated kv back into cache (growing the kv cache). So as models are able to think longer, they need larger context sizes, increasing demand on memory as the model thinks
  - [groq 3](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/inside-nvidia-groq-3-lpx-the-low-latency-inference-accelerator-for-the-nvidia-vera-rubin-platform/)
    - LPUs in general, which are optimized for sequential portion of inference whereas the gpus are for the parallel work
  - [kv caching](https://huggingface.co/blog/not-lain/kv-caching)
  - groq 3 vs vera rubin cpus. rubin is for high throughput, groq is for low latency.
    - [groq tradeoffs](https://newsletter.semianalysis.com/p/groq-inference-tokenomics-speed-but)
QUESTION: [Why is each architecture better in each parameter]
ANSWER: [answer it]
QUESTION: [Why is latency and throughput enemies and going against each other. Why cant make one that is optimized for both?]
ANSWER: [answer it]
  - ![Screenshot 2026-03-16 at 8 15 17 PM](/notes/images/Screenshot_2026-03-16_at_8_15_17_PM.png)

- Understand agentic ai workflows, what operations are most common, and why these supercomputers are architected the way that they are to accelerate these workflows.
- disaggregated inference

notes.title: 2026 Social Media Content Creation
notes.content:
# 2026 Social Media Content Creation

- SPCL
   - Status: control or have access to a scarce resource that other people want. Ex) having lots of money means you have a resource being money that everyone wants
   - Power: saying something that when someone complies, leads to something positive. Ex) someone says to buy 10 stocks, and you do and you make lots of moeny
   - Credibility: proof of acomplishment
   - Likeliness/relatability: how relatable you are to someone else and how alike are you. Ex) vibe with each other, personalities match, same gender, same similar values (want to get rich).

- Social media is moving towards interests and what people find most interesting (all social media algorithms to maximize watch time and time spent on app)

- strategy for 2026 is to make 10 pieces of content per day. Either livestream (best) or make a long video (second best), then make shorts from the longer video to draw people to watch the livestream/long video on youtube.
- make interesting videos and will keep watching

notes.title: Caches
notes.content:
# Caches - Appendix B

- Set-associative usually used in GPUs:
  - Set-associative basically good for many threads since many threads=many different sets of reused code at different parts in the program
  - GPUs run thousands of threads at once, and each thread accesses different pieces of data. If the cache were direct-mapped, many threads might try to store their data in the same cache line, causing conflicts and evictions. Set-associative caches let each “set” hold multiple lines, so threads accessing different data can coexist in the cache without constantly kicking each other out. This reduces conflicts and improves performance.
- Instruction cache and data cache are separated for performance since pipeline accesses instruction in fetch stage and data in ex stage at same time, which cache only has one port causing structural hazard
- Cache optimizations - Appendix B2
  - Hierarchy of cache (L1,L2) with increasing size allows for more chance of cache hit since larger cache increases probability (decreasing miss penalty)

notes.title: Fundamentals of Quantitative Design and Analysis
notes.content:
# Chapter 1: Fundamentals of Quantitative Design and Analysis

-   Moores law: #transistors on microchip double every two years. This has been slowing down recently
-   Amdahl's law: performance of multicore processors, increasing # cores eventually leads to diminishing returns![image](/notes/images/image.png)
-   IoT devices are embedded computers that are connected to internet
-   Servers mainly focus on scalability (how much can I scale my server based on increase in demand), high throughput to serve all customers
-   Clusters are collections of servers into one big computer
    -   **Basically we are aiming to maximize throughput efficiently, so as much throughput as possible with the lowest power consumption/cost** (doing the same work for cheaper). Throughput here is defined by:
        -   **Compute capacity** (number of cores/ALUs/FPUs)
        -   **Memory bandwidth** (can data feed the compute units fast enough, **Feed pipe**?)
        -   **Interconnect bandwidth** (GPU-to-GPU or node-to-node communication-**nvlink**)
        -   **Parallelism exploited** (ILP, thread-level, data-level, model parallelism)
    -   Important to be best in price-performance (lowest inference cost/ price per token-nvidia vera rubin focuses on this since the cost reduction saves millions).
    -   Memory bandwidth (highly parallel systems are bounded by memory bandwidth - feed system with large enough pipe)
-   Parallelism at different levels of abstraction
    -   Data level parallelism (SIMD has hundreds of workers/ALUs doing the same task on multiple data)-same action done on multiple pieces of data. 
    -   Instruction level parallelism (superscalar/out of order exec/pipelining (exec multiple independent instructions at same time)): instructions are independent of each other and can be done in parallel
    -   Thread Level Parallelism (Simultaneous multithreading SMT): running multiple threads on different cores in parallel
-   **Computer Architecture** refers to ISA, microarch, and hardware.
    -   Microarch is the architecture and organization of the computer (design of the system)
    -   Hardware is physical implementation of microarch. Better hardware with same microarch can lead to better performance (**Better hardware** → faster transistors, higher clock speeds, bigger/faster caches, better memory system, more cores, or more reliable packaging.)
-   Architecting with new technology/scalability in mind is important
    -   Also taking into account the fact that bandwidth is scaling by x^2 compared to latency (ML and graphics applications are integral to this trend since bandwidth throttles throughput and throughput is extremely important for AI workloads due to the extremely high quantity of data processing needed)
-   Bandwidth is theoretical, throughput is actual
-   We can no longer improve performance mainly by increasing clock frequency or simply packing more transistors onto a chip, because power and heat limits prevent us from using them all simultaneously. So modern computer architecture improves performance through architectural techniques instead of raw scaling such as **parallelization, ASIC, energy efficient design**
-   Modern AI hardware design is about **maximizing throughput per dollar and per watt while keeping chip size and datacenter power costs manageable**
-   Modern AI/ML and graphics performance is primarily evaluated by **throughput, latency, energy efficiency, cost efficiency, and memory bandwidth**, with **throughput and performance per watt** usually being the most critical because i want my models running as fast as possible (highest throughput) and as cheaply as possible (highest margin).
-   Focus on common case when designing. Make common case fast, and infrequent case

notes.title: Growing instagram
notes.content:
# Growing instagram

-   Have a niche
-   Find trending content in the niche, make 10 videos with a few different variations of each, test them, find a pattern between the top 3 performing ones, keep posting that pattern
-   Hook should be tailored towards grabbing the interest of your niche. I watched a video ad because I was interested in them since I am competing against them

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JLfkBRRvtPc](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JLfkBRRvtPc)

-   Like and save content in my niche to train account to show me only that niche
-   Find trending audio first, edit around it
-   Add polls and engage with audience![image](/notes/images/image-1.png)
-   Caption: ![](https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0?ui=2&ik=71d65d317f&attid=0.2&permmsgid=msg-f:1859508410103707478&th=19ce4d0c01153756&view=fimg&fur=ip&permmsgid=msg-f:1859508410103707478&sz=s0-l75-ft&attbid=ANGjdJ_z15mARHQTSCgfw2Pvj17xDtBI9oG-HaDbCuGZoMhd8lV1azffnErFRhNghWpqHI3wqeRlxITEFx4RFwU5la84fVhfEHUDzOJ0NNHdD2tavs211bXpbqgXAvg&disp=emb&zw)
-   Pin CTA on top performing reels
    -   Use bio to explain why they should follow

-   Use keywords that relate to niche, describe account (what I do), value I provide and why should follow, and community (join the community)

-   Put keywords on videos
-   Core audience needs to find interesting, valuable, entertaining

-   Insta algo:

    -   Pushes out incrementally to more people based on completion time/watch time/retention/replays, shares, saves, comments, likes, follows after watching
    -   Compares performance to other reels that were tested in that niche, if good then pushes further
    -   Audience match targeting based on captions and video transcription

notes.title: Instruction Set Architecture
notes.content:
# ISA - Appendix A {1-8}

  - ISA architecture directly affects computer arch
    - RISC vs CISC: RISC uses simpler, uniform instructions → simpler pipelines, easier decoding, higher throughput.
    - Instruction encoding:
      - Fixed-length (e.g., 32-bit) → simpler fetch/decode, better pipeline performance.
      - Variable-length (e.g., 8/16/32-bit) → smaller code size but more complex decoding hardware.
      - Code size vs performance tradeoff: variable encoding improves memory efficiency; fixed encoding improves execution speed.
    - Compilers strongly affect performance because they determine instruction scheduling, register allocation, and how efficiently the ISA is used.
    - Supported data types/specialized instructions: vector/SIMD for ML applications

notes.title: Markdown Guide
notes.content:
# Markdown Guide

A quick reference for Markdown syntax supported in Notes.

## Q/As

QUESTION'''[This is a question]'''
ANSWER'''[And this is its answer]'''

## Text Formatting

**Bold**, *italic*, ~~strikethrough~~, `inline code`

## Headings

Use `#` through `######` for headings.

## Lists

- Unordered item
- Another item
  - Nested item

1. Ordered item
2. Second item

## Links

- External: [OpenAI](https://openai.com)
- Wiki link to another note: [[Welcome to Notes]]

## Code Blocks

```js
const greet = (name) => `Hello, ${name}!`
console.log(greet('world'))
```

## Blockquotes

> This is a blockquote.

## Tables

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| Cell 1   | Cell 2   |
| Cell 3   | Cell 4   |

## Horizontal Rule

---

Back to [[Welcome to Notes]].

notes.title: Marketing Management
notes.content:
# [Marketing Management](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/15-810-marketing-management-fall-2010/)

- [https://www.bssve.in/StudyMaterials/MB029.pdf](https://www.bssve.in/StudyMaterials/MB029.pdf)

- It is best to assume that other people are not similar to me. For example, just because I have a problem, dont assume others have the same problem.

  - Figure this out through real data from customers, not intuition

-   Overconfidence: thinking im more right than I actually am
-   How customers think

  -   Standard economic approach: selecting best option based on ratio of value to price. I know this best since I am a cheapass.

  -   Motivational approach: decision made by less rational thinking and unconscious or uncontrollable desire (what they REALLY want) and emotions. Such as me wanting to purchase a sports car since its cool luxurious and fast, even though I dont really need it![image  22](/notes/images/image_22_.png)

  -   Behavioral approach: dont look into every detail and rather purchase because of behavioral patterns such as taking shortcuts and not wanting to do all of the heavy research to see if its the best option available. For example, I buy iphone because its popular and everyone has it so it must be a pretty good option, or I go to the restaurant with higher ratings because it should be better than ones with lower ratings without looking at any reviews.![image  23](/notes/images/image_23_.png)


-   Reference price: how much im willing to pay for something, regardless of how much it actually costs. This digs into how much value it provides for me, how much I want it, regardless of how much it actually costs.
- Framing gains vs losses: depending on how you framed things as being a gain or a loss, it changes how people decide. We want to base this on the customers behavior and what they lean more towards.

  - ![image  24](/notes/images/image_24_.png)
  - ![unnamed  1](/notes/images/unnamed_1_.png)
  -   ![image  25](/notes/images/image_25_.png)
  -   When things are framed as gains, we tend to try to minimize the risk, they prefer **sure smaller gains** over risky big gains
  -   whereas framed as losses we tend to seek the risk. they prefer **risky options** to avoid sure losses

notes.title: Modern CPU Concepts, Definitions, and more
notes.content:
# Modern CPU Concepts, Definitions, and more

## [NVIDIA Vera CPU](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/vera-cpu/)

## Concepts and Definitions

### AI
- Edge AI: running AI algorithms, such as neural networks, on local hardware (edge of a network) rather than running through the cloud. For example, running computer vision models right on a smartphone via accelerated hardware.
  
QUESTION [How does hardware process information through a neural network?]
ANSWER [### The 4-Step Hardware Process

1.  **The Math (GEMM):** Processing a layer is just a massive General Matrix Multiplication (GEMM) between the model's **Weights** and the incoming **Activations** (inputs).
    
2.  **Tiling:** Because these matrices are hundreds of gigabytes, the compiler chops them into tiny blocks called **Tiles** so they can fit into the processor's microscopic, ultra-fast hardware registers.
    
3.  **Dataflow (Weight Stationary):** To prevent clogging the memory bandwidth, the chip loads a single Tile of Weights into the registers and *locks them in place*. It then streams thousands of Activations past those locked weights to squeeze out every possible calculation before fetching the next Tile from the slow main memory.
    
4.  **Systolic Arrays:** Modern AI chips (like NVIDIA Tensor Cores) use a 2D grid of Arithmetic Logic Units (ALUs). Instead of ALUs constantly reading/writing to memory registers, data physically flows directly from one ALU to its neighbor like a factory assembly line.
    

---

### A Concrete Example: Processing an Image

Imagine a neural network layer has **1,000,000 weights**, but your GPU’s physical registers can only hold **1,000 weights** at a time.

1.  **Tiling & Loading:** The GPU grabs "Tile 1" (the first 1,000 weights) from the slow off-chip memory (HBM) and loads them into the physical ALU registers.
    
2.  **Locking it Down:** Using a Weight Stationary dataflow, the GPU locks those 1,000 weights in the registers.
    
3.  **The Assembly Line:** The GPU takes your entire input image (millions of pixels) and streams it through the Systolic Array. Pixel A is multiplied by Weight 1, the running total is handed directly to the neighboring ALU to add Weight 2, and so on. The weights never move; only the pixel data flows through the grid.
    
4.  **The Swap:** Once the entire image has been multiplied against Tile 1, the GPU finally clears the registers, fetches "Tile 2" (the next 1,000 weights) from the slow memory, and streams the image through again.
    

This hyper-efficient loop prevents the chip from constantly wasting time and power moving weights back and forth from the main memory.]


### Hardware
- KV cache: key-value cache is a crucial technique for accelerating Large Language Model (LLM) inference by storing the keys and values of previous tokens in memory
- [HPC High performance compute](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/glossary/high-performance-computing/)
- Spatial Multithreading: multithreading by statically allocating hardware for each thread.
QUESTION: [This still makes no sense since to me, dynamic allocation would be better since we aim to utilize all functional units whereas static allocation we might have functional units that are unused if that thread doesnt contain such operations.]
ANSWER: [Here is a concrete example of how this happens during an actual AI training run.

Imagine you are training a massive AI model like ChatGPT, and the workload is split evenly across all 72 GPUs in the rack. AI training operates in highly synchronized, rigid steps.

-   **Step 1 (Compute):** All 72 GPUs crunch their specific chunk of the math simultaneously.
    
-   **Step 2 (Synchronize):** Before anyone can move on, all 72 GPUs must swap their answers with each other to update the overall AI model (an operation called "All-Reduce").
    
-   **Step 3 (Next Batch):** The CPU feeds the next batch of data to the GPUs to start the cycle over.
    

**The SMT Failure Scenario:** Imagine CPU Thread 12 is responsible for feeding data to GPU #12. In a traditional SMT setup, Thread 12 dynamically shares a processing lane with Thread 13.

Suddenly, Thread 13 needs to do a heavy calculation. It hogs the core's Arithmetic Logic Unit. Thread 12 is forced to wait at a microscopic red light for just 2 milliseconds.

**The Domino Effect:**

1.  Because of that 2-millisecond CPU traffic jam, GPU #12 receives its data late.
    
2.  GPU #12 finishes its math 2 milliseconds behind everyone else.
    
3.  **The Halt:** The other 71 GPUs finish perfectly on time, but they hit a brick wall at Step 2. They literally cannot swap their answers and move to Step 3 until GPU #12 shows up with its homework.
    

Those 71 massive, incredibly expensive GPUs sit completely frozen, burning power and doing zero math, entirely because one CPU thread had to share a lane.

By using Spatial Multithreading, NVIDIA gives Thread 12 its own dedicated, physically walled-off lane. It never hits a red light, GPU #12 never falls behind, and all 72 GPUs fire in perfect unison 100% of the time.]
- Arithmetic Intensity: measures how many math operations (FLOPs) are performed for every byte of data moved from memory. Higher arithmetic intensity is better since higher ratio means more computational throughput with minimal data movement.
QUESTION [What is the biggest bottleneck in AI inference compute right now]
ANSWER[## Gemini said

You are hearing correctly: **memory bandwidth is absolutely the biggest bottleneck** for modern AI inference, particularly for Large Language Models (LLMs).

To understand why this is true—and how engineers mathematically prove it—we have to look at how these models actually generate text and the physical limits of the hardware they run on.

### Why Memory Bandwidth is the Bottleneck

AI inference for LLMs happens in two distinct phases, and only the second phase suffers from the memory bottleneck:

1.  **The Prefill Phase (Reading the Prompt):** When you send a massive prompt to an AI, the GPU processes all those words at once in parallel. This phase is heavily math-intensive. The compute units (ALUs/Tensor Cores) are maxed out. This phase is **compute-bound**.
    
    +1
    
2.  **The Decode Phase (Generating the Answer):** This is where the AI types out its answer one word (token) at a time. To predict *just one single word*, the GPU has to read the **entire neural network** (every single weight and parameter, which can be hundreds of gigabytes) from its memory chips into its processor cores. It does a tiny bit of math, spits out one word, and then has to *reload the entire model all over again* for the next word.
    

Because the GPU has to constantly shove massive amounts of data through the "pipes" connecting the memory to the processor just to do a tiny bit of math, those pipes get clogged. The processor ends up twiddling its thumbs waiting for data to arrive. This phase is **memory bandwidth-bound**.

### How Do We Actually Know This? (The Proof)

Engineers don't just guess that memory is the bottleneck; they prove it using strict mathematical frameworks and real-world hardware profiling.

**1\. Arithmetic Intensity (The Math)** Computer scientists use a metric called **Arithmetic Intensity**, which measures how many math operations (FLOPs) are performed for every byte of data moved from memory.

-   Modern GPUs can perform hundreds of trillions of math operations per second (TeraFLOPs).
    
-   However, their memory bandwidth is usually only a few Terabytes per second (TB/s).
    
-   During the token generation phase, the arithmetic intensity is incredibly low (often close to 1 or 2 math operations per byte loaded). Because the GPU's ability to do math vastly outpaces its ability to fetch data, the math units sit idle.
    

**2\. The Roofline Model** Hardware architects use a visual tool called the **Roofline Model** to diagnose bottlenecks. It graphs a processor's peak compute performance against its peak memory bandwidth. When you map an LLM's decode phase onto this graph, the workload slams directly into the "slanted roof" of the chart, which represents the physical limit of the memory bandwidth, long before it ever reaches the "flat roof" of the compute limit.

**3\. Hardware Utilization Profiling** If you run an NVIDIA or AMD profiler during LLM inference, the physical metrics prove it. You will see the memory bandwidth utilization pegged at nearly 100%, while the actual compute units (the Tensor Cores) are sitting at 5% to 10% utilization. The brain is ready to think, but it can't read the book fast enough.

### The Hardware Industry's Reaction

The ultimate proof that memory bandwidth is the bottleneck is where the hardware industry is spending its billions:

-   **HBM (High Bandwidth Memory):** NVIDIA and AMD are stacking extremely expensive, advanced memory chips directly next to the GPU silicon to widen the data pipes.
    
-   **Groq and Cerebras:** These companies have abandoned traditional memory entirely. They build massive chips filled entirely with ultra-fast SRAM (cache) so the entire model lives directly next to the compute units, eliminating the memory transit time completely.
    

Would you like to explore how software engineers use techniques like **KV Caching** or **Quantization** to artificially shrink the model so it requires less memory bandwidth?]

- [Systolic Arrays](https://ecelabs.njit.edu/ece459/lab3.php): basically a hardware architecture that is optimized for matrix multiplications that physically wires the functional units into a matrix where each output is handed down to the next. This increases **arithmentic intensity** because we do less movement of memory by handing down through hardware itself.
![image](/notes/images/image-2.png)
![image](/notes/images/image-3.png)

notes.title: Pipelineing
notes.content:
# Pipelining - Appendix C


- Pipelining takes advantage of parallelism between the actions needed to execute instructions (washing machine/assembly line analogy)
  - Pipelining increases the processor instruction throughput—the number of instructions completed per unit of time—but it does not reduce the execution time of an individual instruction. In fact, it usually slightly increases the execution time of each instruction due to overhead in the control of the pipeline. The increase in instruction throughput means that a program runs faster and has lower total execution time, even though no single instruction runs faster!

notes.content:
# List of prompts and other stuff that I use with AI

## Prompts for software dev and programming

notes.title: Sales and business 101
Transformers
notes.content:
# Machine Learning Transformer

## [Transformers]([https://medium.com/@amanatulla1606/transformer-architecture-explained-2c49e2257b4c](https://medium.com/@amanatulla1606/transformer-architecture-explained-2c49e2257b4c)): Neural network architecture with attention to context
![image  13](/notes/images/image_13_.png)
![image  14](/notes/images/image_14_.png)

 **Tokenization**: converts the input into tokens, which are known words, phrases, punctuation, ext. Basically breaks it down to the fundamental building blocks of that sentence![Tokenization](/notes/images/unnamed.png)
 
 **Embedding**: converting the token into its semantic, basically representing its meaning as a vector of numbers![image  15](/notes/images/image_15_.png)
- To convert the token to embedding, we use a token to embedding table (table of learned embeddings of each word). For unknown token, we can divide the token even further and embed it that way.

**Positional encoding**: adding a positional vector to each word to basically embed the position of that word within the entire sentence![image  17](/notes/images/image_17_.png)

**Transformer block**: a block/layer of the neural network![image  16](/notes/images/image_16_.png)

**[Attention]([https://towardsdatascience.com/transformers-explained-visually-part-3-multi-head-attention-deep-dive-1c1ff1024853/](https://towardsdatascience.com/transformers-explained-visually-part-3-multi-head-attention-deep-dive-1c1ff1024853/))** - part of a transformer block: giving context to each word where the context is the context of the entire phrase or sentence or text piece. Basically relating each token with the rest of the phrase![image  18](/notes/images/image_18_.png)
**Softmax layer**: after the embeddings go through the network, the output of the transformer/NN is the final embedding of the token, the embedding then gets converted/projected into the vocabulary space (all possible next words) where each component of this vocabulary vector is called a logit, then we use softmax layer to convert those logits into probabilities, which is the probability of that vocabulary word to be the next word in the sentence.![image  19](/notes/images/image_19_.png)


## [Attention]([https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eMlx5fFNoYc](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eMlx5fFNoYc)):Example: say I want to know if I am similar to another person
  - Q: query: basically the question/query/way that a token asks all other tokens to see if they are related in that specific way. In the example here, the Query here could be a multi-dimensional profile of what I’m looking for (height, weight, personality, etc.), that I can then compare with other peoples profile to see if I am similar to them or not. In the context of the transformer, this is basically a matrix that represents that question/query
  - Key: the profiles of all other people that I want to compare with. By taking the dot product of my profile (Q) with everyone else's profile (K), I can see the similarity I have with each of them. We can think of the key as the answer to the query, and how similar the query is to the key.
  - Value: What information do I take from each person once I decide they’re relevant? From example, this value can be that the value is the useful information from those similar people that I can use to learn from them. For example, If I want to learn about transformers, I can find people online that are similar to me, then use their knowledge to learn more about transformers. The value is the actual content that updates my knowledge and understanding.
![image  21](/notes/images/image_21_.png)

Questions:
-   How is it possible to run so many requests on one NN at a time. In the context of Chatgpt or any other large provider, how to they handle all of these requests at one time? Are they all queued then sequentially ran, and just because they are so fast it seems like they are happening concurrnetly?

## [Transformer Decode stage](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ekg-hoob0SM)

## Questions:
-   How each step of the transformer runs on hardware, especially how it can be parallelized, how it is distributed across SMs, how data is moved, and how data is aggregated and synchronized at the end and during computation

notes.title: Verilator
notes.content:
# q1 verilator install

- Verilator basically compiles verilog code into c++ so that it can run much faster than other simulators
- When top->eval() is called, this basically halts main, and runs the verilog c++ thread with all updated variables. Its important to note that verilator optimizes c++ code so sometimes updates wil l still occur in hardware even if eval is not called since behind the scenes, things like assign/wires are just references so they update automatically even without eval being called.

notes.title: Welcome to Notes
notes.content:
# Welcome to Notes

This is your personal knowledge base. You can write notes in **Markdown** and link them together.

## Features

- **Folders** — organize notes into nested folders
- **Topics** — tag notes for cross-folder filtering
- **[[Wiki Links]]** — link notes together with `[[Note Title]]`
- **Backlinks** — see which notes reference the current one
- **Search** — quickly find any note

## Getting Started

Create a new note with the **+** button next to any folder. Add tags to cross-reference across folders.

Try linking to [[Markdown Guide]] to see how wiki-style links work.
